items.explanation:
21st President (1881–1885), Republican
Nicknamed "The Gentleman Boss" for his elegant style and political connections
Assumed the presidency after James Garfield's assassination
Surprised everyone by championing civil service reform — signed the Pendleton Act despite being a product of the spoils system himself
Accused of being born in Canada rather than Vermont — an early "birther" controversy
Known for his lavish wardrobe and redecorating the White House with Tiffany glass and new furnishings
Had been a machine politician running the New York Customs House before the vice presidency
Suffered from Bright's disease (kidney failure) which he kept secret from the public

22nd and 24th President — the only president to serve non-consecutive terms
Only Democrat elected president between 1856 and 1912
Vetoed more bills than all previous presidents combined
Secretly had surgery for oral cancer on a friend's yacht in 1893 to avoid causing a market panic — the public didn't learn of it for decades
Known for personal honesty — "Tell the truth" was his guiding motto
Married Frances Folsom in the White House in 1886 — the only presidential wedding held there — making her the youngest First Lady at age 21
The Baby Ruth candy bar may have been named for his daughter Ruth, though this is disputed

23rd President (1889–1893), Republican
Grandson of William Henry Harrison (#9) — the only grandparent-grandchild pair in presidential history
Nicknamed "Little Ben" at just 5'6" tall
First president with electricity in the White House, but he and his wife were reportedly afraid to touch the switches
Signed the Sherman Antitrust Act (1890), the first federal law to limit monopolies
Admitted six states during his term: both Dakotas, Montana, Washington, Idaho, and Wyoming
Presided over the first "billion-dollar Congress" due to record federal spending
Lost the popular vote to Grover Cleveland in both 1888 (won Electoral College) and 1892 (lost both)

Same person as #22 — the only president to serve non-consecutive terms
Second term dominated by the Panic of 1893, the worst economic depression until the 1930s
Sent federal troops to break the Pullman Strike in 1894 without the request of the Illinois governor
Repealed the Sherman Silver Purchase Act to stabilize the gold standard
Lost the support of his own Democratic Party by the end of his second term
Left office deeply unpopular, with the country still mired in economic crisis

25th President (1897–1901), Republican
Last Civil War veteran to serve as president
Led the nation into the Spanish-American War (1898), gaining Puerto Rico, Guam, and the Philippines
Presided over U.S. annexation of Hawaii (1898)
Assassinated by anarchist Leon Czolgosz at the Pan-American Exposition in Buffalo, New York (September 1901)
His campaign pioneered the "front porch" strategy, delivering speeches from his home in Canton, Ohio
His face was on the $500 bill
His death elevated Theodore Roosevelt to the presidency

26th President (1901–1909), Republican
Youngest person to become president at age 42, after McKinley's assassination
Won the Nobel Peace Prize for mediating the Russo-Japanese War (1905) — first American Nobel laureate
Championed the national parks system and conservation, protecting roughly 230 million acres of public land
Built the Panama Canal, one of the greatest engineering feats of the era
"Trust Buster" who broke up monopolies under the Sherman Antitrust Act
The "Teddy Bear" was named after him following a hunting trip incident
Left the Republican Party to run as the Bull Moose (Progressive) candidate in 1912
Shot during a 1912 campaign speech in Milwaukee — finished the speech with the bullet still in his chest
One of four faces on Mount Rushmore

27th President (1909–1913), Republican
Only person in U.S. history to serve as both President and Chief Justice of the Supreme Court (appointed 1921)
Weighed over 300 pounds — the heaviest president in American history
Popular legend says he got stuck in the White House bathtub, though the story may be apocryphal
Handpicked by Theodore Roosevelt as his successor, but they became bitter political rivals — Roosevelt ran against him in 1912
His administration actually filed more antitrust suits than Roosevelt's, despite TR getting the "Trust Buster" label
Reportedly said the presidency was "the loneliest place in the world" — he much preferred the judiciary
First president to throw a ceremonial first pitch at a baseball game (1910)

28th President (1913–1921), Democrat
Led the nation through World War I after initially campaigning on keeping the U.S. out of the war
Proposed the League of Nations and won the Nobel Peace Prize (1919), but the U.S. Senate rejected American membership
Signed the Federal Reserve Act (1913), creating the modern central banking system
Signed the 19th Amendment (1920) granting women the right to vote
Suffered a severe stroke in October 1919 — his wife Edith effectively ran the executive branch for the remaining months of his presidency
Re-segregated the federal workforce, reversing progress made since Reconstruction
Only president with a PhD (political science, Johns Hopkins) and former president of Princeton University

29th President (1921–1923), Republican
Won in a landslide promising a "return to normalcy" after the upheaval of World War I
Presidency plagued by the Teapot Dome scandal — his Interior Secretary Albert Fall took bribes in exchange for oil drilling leases on federal land
Died suddenly in San Francisco in August 1923 — the cause was debated (likely a heart attack or stroke)
Appointed the first female director of the U.S. Mint (Nellie Tayloe Ross came later; he appointed the first female Bureau chief)
Coined the word "normalcy" as a political slogan, though the word already existed in English
Often ranked among the worst presidents by historians
His extramarital affair with Nan Britton, long rumored, was confirmed by DNA testing in 2015

30th President (1923–1929), Republican
Nicknamed "Silent Cal" — famous for being a man of few words
Sworn in by his own father, a notary public, by kerosene lamp at 2:47 AM after Harding's death in 1923
Presided over the Roaring Twenties economic boom and embraced laissez-faire economics
Famously declared "the chief business of the American people is business"
Vetoed a farm relief bill (McNary-Haugen) twice, angering agricultural states
Chose not to run for reelection in 1928, announcing simply "I do not choose to run for president in 1928"
His 16-year-old son Calvin Jr. died in 1924 from an infected blister he got playing tennis on the White House grounds — Coolidge said the presidency lost its appeal after the loss
